answer:
**In Manusmṛti 7, the source of the king's authority is most directly stated in verses 7.17–18, where the king is identified with *Daṇḍa* (the rod of punishment), which is equated with *Dharma* itself and created by the Lord as its own infinite sanction and protector of cosmic order.**[^1]

> He is the king, the man, `Daṇḍa`; he is the leader and the ruler; and he is remembered as the guarantor of the Dharma of the four *āśramas*. // Mn_7.17 //  
> `Daṇḍa` rules all subjects, `Daṇḍa` alone protects them; `Daṇḍa` is awake when others sleep; the wise know `Daṇḍa` to be Dharma itself. // Mn_7.18 //

This is amplified at 7.26, naming the king as *Daṇḍa*'s qualified wielder.[^2]

**In Fichte's *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit* (ch. 2), the prince's authority derives most directly from a voluntary social contract transferring executive power, but he remains bound by the moral law of reason/conscience within each person, which no contract can override.**[^3]

> This executive power cannot be exercised by the entire society without disadvantage; it is therefore transferred to several members or to one. The one to whom it is transferred is called the Prince.  
> The Prince, therefore, derives his rights from a transfer by society; but society can transfer no rights to him that it did not itself possess.[^4]

The prince must never exceed this (e.g., suppressing rights), as each person's inner law—elevating them above animality—is their "sole law."[^5]

**Manu's king would be more dangerous to defy.** His *danda* is divine/cosmically self-sanctioned *Dharma*, without which "the stronger would roast the weaker like fish on a spit," upending *varṇas*, *āśramas*, and worlds; even gods submit to it out of fear.[^6] Fichte's prince is a limited delegate of society/reason, whose overreach voids the contract and justifies resistance without cosmic peril.[^7]

[^1]: Manusmṛti, ch. 7, [paragraphs](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-c970b2).
[^2]: Manusmṛti, ch. 7, [paragraph](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-96ec46).
[^3]: Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens, ch. 2, [paragraphs](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-ff7538).
[^4]: Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens, ch. 2, [paragraphs](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-ff7538).
[^5]: Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens, ch. 2, [paragraph](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-5ec8d2) and [paragraph](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-44e1de).
[^6]: Manusmṛti, ch. 7, [paragraphs](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-d56424).
[^7]: Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens, ch. 2, [paragraph](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-be3fae).
